title: SEGMENT 17 — The competition: 3D bioprinting and stem-cell organs

lines.text: SEGMENT 17 — The competition: 3D bioprinting and stem-cell organs
Xeno is not the only contender for the title of "future source of transplantable organs.
" There are at least two other approaches that are sometimes presented as alternatives.
Let me cover them briefly, because the comparison matters.
The first is 3D bioprinting.
The idea, which has been around since roughly the early 2000s, is that you can use a modified 3D printer to deposit layers of living cells, suspended in a hydrogel scaffold, in the shape of an organ.
The cells could come from the patient themselves — autologous, eliminating the immune problem entirely — or from a universal donor cell line.
Over time, the printed scaffold would mature into a functional organ.
The progress has been real but slow.
Bioprinted skin, bioprinted cartilage, bioprinted bladder — these have been demonstrated, and in a few cases used clinically.
The challenge for solid organs is vascularization.
A kidney has roughly a million functional units called nephrons, each with its own intricate capillary network, all hooked up to a central vascular tree.
Printing the macro-anatomy is the easy part.
Recreating the dense, hierarchical microvasculature that delivers blood to every cell is the hard part.
Without functional vasculature, the inner cells of the print starve and die.
There are companies — including United Therapeutics' own subsidiary 3D Bio-Therapeutics, and others like Prellis Biologics and Aspect Biosystems — working on this problem.
Recent advances in printing speed and scaffold chemistry have closed some of the gap.
But no bioprinted solid organ has yet been transplanted into a human.
The field is, by most estimates, ten to twenty years behind xeno.
The second alternative is stem-cell-derived organs, sometimes called organoids scaled up, or iPSC-derived tissues.
Induced pluripotent stem cells — iPSCs — can be coaxed in culture to develop into miniature versions of organs: kidney organoids, liver organoids, cardiac organoids.
These mini-organs are useful for research and drug screening.
The question is whether they can be scaled up to a transplantable size and complexity.
So far, mostly no.
Organoids are millimeter-scale.
A functional kidney is the size of a fist.
The developmental cues needed to take a millimeter-scale organoid up to organ scale are not yet well understood, and we don't know how to deliver them in a culture dish.
Some progress has been made — there are papers on growing scaled-up bits of intestine, on producing pancreatic islets that can be implanted to treat diabetes.
But a transplantable solid organ grown from iPSCs has not been achieved.
There is, however, a hybrid idea that bridges xeno and stem-cell approaches.

The idea is interspecies chimerism: implant human iPSCs into a pig embryo, with the pig embryo modified so that it cannot develop its own pancreas (for example), and let the human cells fill in the missing organ.
The result is a pig that grows a human pancreas.
The pancreas is then harvested for transplant.
This sounds like science fiction, but it has been demonstrated in proof of principle.
Several labs — including teams at Stanford, the Salk Institute, and in Japan — have produced pig embryos with partial human contribution.
The technique is called blastocyst complementation.
It has not yet produced a transplantable human organ inside a pig.
But it is a real direction, and it would solve some of xeno's immune problems by making the donor organ literally human in cellular composition.
So the field's competition is real but distant.
For the next decade, the most plausible source of large-scale transplantable organs is edited pigs.
Bioprinting and stem-cell-derived organs may eventually surpass xeno, but xeno is the one that's in clinical use today.
That's why we've spent two hours on it.
